l443: Verified Professional Certification • Industry Standard for PHP Engineering
l450: Started at 14 with a Delphi book and no internet. By college, I was neck-deep in Pascal, VBA, Firebird, and hacking the lab's WinServer 2x network. My first big project? A WoW server (MaNGOS) on C++ where I learned the pain of patching binary files.
l451: The real fun started with Lineage 2. I built a custom Socks4/5 proxy layer to intercept and modify packets on-the-fly. It was pure raw socket magic—completely invisible to anti-cheat systems. Later, I built 0-day exploits for Firefox and Adobe Reader by finding crashes via Google and spending nights in WinDbg/OllyDbg.
l453: Developing botnets taught me extreme optimization. I integrated a Lua interpreter into a C-core, stripped the parser, and fed it raw bytecode—the result was a 4KB binary. Try doing that with Python or PHP!
l454: I built my own custom cryptor based on a Python-to-Pseudocode translator with binary tree algorithms and heavy obfuscation. I even discovered that bypassing Kaspersky back then was possible just by using CMD/FTP for shellcode instead of standard WinAPI—it simply didn't hook the command line the same way.
l457: Debian is the only stable choice. Use UDP instead of TCP wherever real-time performance matters.
l459: Foreign Keys are evil (except for billing). Stop the subquery madness and stop treating the DB like a trash can. Use Redis/Memcached and proper composite indexes.
l461: Thin clients (PHP) => High-speed Microservices (Go/Java Spring). No monoliths. I even designed a fully decentralized P2P trust ring that is impossible to shut down.
l463: Once spent 24 hours debugging a broken auth system. It turned out Pinba was corrupting the virtual function stack, shifting it by a few bytes. The interpreter was literally calling random methods. That’s why I don’t trust "magic" monitoring tools anymore.
l464: "I love computers, I understand them, and they understand me."
